") [Versions Notes](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22/notes) Highlights ---------- **What are the main findings?** * Inclusiveness, stakeholder collaboration, sustainability, governance, and public satisfaction are crucial to achieving resilience in smart cities. * Strategic planning and integrated urban systems, requiring interdisciplinary work and public engagement are essential for fostering urban resilience. **What are the implications of the main findings?** * Research and practice should acknowledge the need to improve the design and implementation of smart and resilient city concepts. * Urban planners should integrate resilience into policymaking in developing smart and resilient cities. Abstract -------- Smart city planning is crucial for enhancing urban resilience, especially with the contemporary challenges of rising urban population and climate change. This study conducts a systematic literature review (SLR) to examine the integration of urban resilience in smart city planning, synthesizing the current literature to identify key components, barriers, and enablers. The study found that technological integration, sustainability measures, and citizens’ participation are critical factors to the effective development of smart cities. The review emphasizes the need for an integrated approach to urban resilience, calling for continued research and collaboration among stakeholders. It highlights how urban sustainability and resilience should be addressed within an urban system and that interdisciplinary work, stakeholder consultation, and public engagement are required. It finally suggests the integration of creativity and diversity in urban planning practices and policies for improving vulnerability to modern-day challenges in urban contexts. It concludes by outlining implications for urban planning practices and policy development, advocating for innovative, inclusive strategies to enhance urban resilience. Keywords: [urban resilience](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=urban+resilience); [smart cities](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=smart+cities); [sustainability](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=sustainability); [urban planning](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=urban+planning); [governance](https://www.mdpi.com/search?q=governance) 1. Introduction --------------- Smart city development is crucial to building urban resilience, defined as the capacity of urban areas to prevent, mitigate, adapt to, and bounce back from natural, economic, and social disturbances [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B1-smartcities-08-00022)] and continues to function effectively [[2](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B2-smartcities-08-00022)]. Smart cities foster urban resilience by addressing the challenges of providing and managing urban infrastructure [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B3-smartcities-08-00022)]. This is especially so as cities worldwide are faced with the daunting urbanization and climate change challenges and socioeconomic vulnerabilities [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B4-smartcities-08-00022),[5](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B5-smartcities-08-00022)]. Smart city planning deploys technology and big data to better enhance urban management and service delivery to solve urban challenges [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B6-smartcities-08-00022)] and create urban resilience [[7](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B7-smartcities-08-00022)], including the capacity to make adjustments in times of vulnerabilities, such as “teleworking” from home during the COVID-19 pandemic when lockdowns and social distancing disrupted the ability of workers to commute from home to work [[8](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B8-smartcities-08-00022),[9](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B9-smartcities-08-00022)]. Improving urban housing and managing urban density are other resilience policies [[10](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B10-smartcities-08-00022),[11](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B11-smartcities-08-00022)]. Thus, the COVID-19 crisis exposed weaknesses in urban systems and underlined the necessity of integrating resilience principles in urban areas [[12](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B12-smartcities-08-00022),[13](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B13-smartcities-08-00022),[14](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B14-smartcities-08-00022)]. In the post-pandemic era, smart technologies and digital tools can facilitate urban resilience, planning and management decision-making, remote jobs, and the provision of essential services such as public health and education [[15](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B15-smartcities-08-00022),[16](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B16-smartcities-08-00022),[17](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B17-smartcities-08-00022)]. Thus, the pandemic experience is vital in developing smart and resilient cities. Although smart city initiatives concern identifying technologies and solutions for building urban resilience, new tools are necessary to effectively detect and combat growing threats [[18](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B18-smartcities-08-00022)]. For example, data protection and cybersecurity are major challenges to smart city planning and urban resilience [[19](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B19-smartcities-08-00022)]. Also, some smart solutions are likely to lead to “tech divides,” when these technologies serve few citizens while deepening social inequalities [[20](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B20-smartcities-08-00022)]. Lack of access to technology or digital skills can also marginalize vulnerable people from fully benefitting from city smartness and jeopardizing overall resilience [[4](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B4-smartcities-08-00022)]. Moreover, these technologies have significant environmental impacts through their high use of energy and resources [[21](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B21-smartcities-08-00022),[22](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B22-smartcities-08-00022),[23](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B23-smartcities-08-00022)]. Other issues are smart cities focusing more on technologies with little involvement of citizens, inadequate governance and coordination among actors in the system, such as government and non-governmental agencies, and firms [[24](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B24-smartcities-08-00022)]. Lack of convergence of efforts and sharing of information and assets among stakeholders have been identified as challenges to building resilience [[25](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B25-smartcities-08-00022),[26](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B26-smartcities-08-00022)]. The literature indicates a dearth of studies on evaluating the impact of smart city initiatives on the resilience of cities, smart city initiatives and changes required to enhance urban resilience, or the success factors and obstacles to the implementation of smart city projects [[27](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B27-smartcities-08-00022),[28](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B28-smartcities-08-00022),[29](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B29-smartcities-08-00022),[30](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B30-smartcities-08-00022)]. There is more to smart city solutions and plans than technology and innovation, as appropriate policies and governance systems are essential in managing and coordinating various actors and strategies involved in smart city development to enhance urban resilience. The combination of the concepts of urban resilience and smart city has important implications for urban planning, including the mitigation of the negative impacts of increasing technology deployment and the integration of resilience and sustainability into urban fabric [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B6-smartcities-08-00022),[31](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B31-smartcities-08-00022),[32](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B32-smartcities-08-00022)]. Despite the expansive literature on urban resilience and smart city development, the knowledge gap yet to be bridged is how resilience and smart city frameworks can be successfully implemented and practiced in an integrated manner. Most of the research work targets resilience or smart city strategies individually while rarely attempting to understand how the two are related and how each may bolster the other. The present literature review aims to systematically assess the extant literature to determine the essential components, drivers, and challenges for urban resilience in smart cities. The research questions guiding this systematic review are (a) Can cities become more resilient while pursuing smart city planning? and (b) What are the challenges and opportunities for the synergy between smart city planning and urban resilience? These research questions aim at understanding the existing research gaps regarding the relationship between urban resilience and smart city development to explore what works and identify enablers and barriers. This article is unique in its systematic analysis of the twin premises of technology and geopolitics within smart, resilient cities. It expands the body of knowledge by providing directions for smart city planning, strategies that can enhance urban resilience, and the potential of smart city solutions for improving the readiness of future cities from a risk management perspective. 2. Materials and Methods ------------------------ #### 2.1. Urban Resilience and Smart City Planning in the Context of Sustainable Urban Development Urban resilience and smart city development are vital components of contemporary city-making, especially in view of challenges such as climate change, rapid urbanization, and socioeconomic transformations. Urban resilience is the ability of urban areas to withstand and bounce back from environmental and socioeconomic shocks and stresses, which is closely connected to the concept of urban sustainability [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B3-smartcities-08-00022)]. Resilient cities refer to cities that do not break down when disturbances occur and quickly recover to their state of functionality [[33](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B33-smartcities-08-00022)]. These definitions encompass several key concepts: adaptability: the ability of an urban system to change its state in response to various influences and undo the negative effects of a disruption [[34](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B34-smartcities-08-00022)]; robustness: the ability to continue to perform at a normal rate and maintain the ability to operate even after an abrupt change [[35](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B35-smartcities-08-00022)]; redundancy: the existence of multiple routes and assets to support vital operations [[36](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B36-smartcities-08-00022)]; flexibility: the adaptability to new circumstances, as the ability to transform state and actions, according to the context of the new problem [[37](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B37-smartcities-08-00022)]; inclusivity: implementing resilience-building initiatives to capture all the facets of the urban populace [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B3-smartcities-08-00022)]. According to Sharifi and Yamagata [[38](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B38-smartcities-08-00022)], resilience can help develop smart city planning frameworks to make urban environments intelligent and resistant to various adversities. On the other hand, a smart city connotes a city that uses information and communication technology (ICT), including big data and AI, to improve the efficiency of services in the city while using resources sustainably and efficiently as well as improving its interactions with the citizens [[30](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B30-smartcities-08-00022)]. Key characteristics of smart cities include the following: technology integration: ubiquitous integration of ICT in urban structures and facilities [[39](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B39-smartcities-08-00022)]; data-driven decision-making: incorporating big data technologies to support and guide the planning and development of urban environments [[40](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B40-smartcities-08-00022)]; connectivity: the wireless means of communication facilitate the effective and efficient transfer of information in real time [[41](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B41-smartcities-08-00022)]; sustainability: concentration on the usage of environmental protection activities and resource conservation [[42](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B42-smartcities-08-00022)]; a citizen-centric approach, which includes the focus on the quality of life and active participation of citizens in the management of the city [[43](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B43-smartcities-08-00022)]. Smart city planning implements advanced technologies in developing and managing cities, optimizing service delivery, and minimizing resource use [[44](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B44-smartcities-08-00022),[45](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B45-smartcities-08-00022)], while enhancing human quality of life and urban resilience and sustainability [[46](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B46-smartcities-08-00022)]. Thus, the integration of “urban resilience” and “smart city” can promote social inclusion and environmental management to enhance the capability of urban centers to address present and future challenges [[1](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B1-smartcities-08-00022)]. Some distinct virtues of smart city planning in creating urban resilience include better systems, data management, real-time communication, and resource utilization [[23](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B23-smartcities-08-00022)]. Because smart city planning varies based on its emphasis on tech, politics, or people, proper adoption of such technologies calls for substantive understanding of human systems in cities and long term planning that includes adequate resilience and innovation [[40](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B40-smartcities-08-00022)]. Sharifi et al. [[9](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B9-smartcities-08-00022)] suggest that the concept cannot be conceived as a product but rather as a process, as it is driven by social, economic, and environmental factors. The above drivers underscore the importance of a holistic view and approach to planning smart cities that incorporate resilience principles to prepare them for the multiple opportunities and challenges the future has in store [[3](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B3-smartcities-08-00022)]. Therefore, the present study adopts the following definition of urban resilience in the context of smart city planning: Smart urban resilience is the ability of an urban system to continue to restore its key functions following shocks or stresses affecting a city and its people, while incorporating ICT and sustainable development [[6](https://www.mdpi.com/2624-6511/8/1/22#B6-smartcities-08-00022)]. Yet, advancements in technology, urban planning, and governance offer promising solutions, enabling cities to become more resilient, inclusive, and efficient in resource management. ![](https://secure.gravatar.com/avatar/3c794cb2a709c621a4c82c62c26ef074a6634a81da511e077d62e3d7466a476d?s=70&d=mm&r=g) As **urbanization accelerates worldwide**, cities face mounting challenges that threaten their **sustainability and resilience**. From climate change and resource depletion to socioeconomic disparities and public health crises, urban areas must navigate complex issues while ensuring a high quality of life for their inhabitants. The COVID-19 pandemic underscored the fragility of urban systems, but it also provided valuable insights into the potential for rapid adaptation and innovation in city planning. This article explores the essential components of sustainable urban development, highlighting best practices from leading cities and the role of governance, technology, and community engagement in shaping resilient and inclusive urban environments. Through a combination of nature-based solutions, climate-conscious design, and forward-thinking policies, the **sustainable cities of the future** can transform challenges into opportunities, paving the way for a more equitable and ecologically responsible urban future. List of Contents ## The Urgency of Sustainable Urban Development A **sustainable city** meets the needs of its current residents without compromising those of future generations. It integrates economic, environmental, and social sustainability to ensure a high quality of life while minimizing ecological impact. The cities of the future must be designed with adaptability in mind, ensuring they are inclusive and environmentally responsible. Key areas of focus include: ![](https://www.uccrn.education/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/sustainable-cities-of-the-future-1.jpg) ## Sustainable Urban Infrastructure and Green Development The COVID-19 pandemic demonstrated how cities can rapidly adapt their structures and behaviors, offering valuable lessons on addressing health and climate crises. Some cities, such as Copenhagen, Singapore, and Curitiba, have already set strong examples by prioritizing **renewable energy**, **sustainable mobility**, **green infrastructure**, and resilient urban planning. Copenhagen, for instance, has developed advanced stormwater management systems that not only mitigate the effects of heavy rainfall but also enhance urban biodiversity and create recreational spaces for residents. Five key features define a sustainable city: Moving forward, cities must adopt comprehensive mitigation and adaptation strategies, invest in green infrastructure, ensure equitable access to resources, and leverage technological innovations to create more efficient and sustainable urban environments. **The time to act is now**—building the sustainable cities of the future is not just an option but a necessity. ![](https://www.uccrn.education/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Screenshot-2025-03-19-133940.png) ## Discover Third Assessment Report on Climate Change and Cities (ARC3.3) Cities and the urbanization process itself are at a crossroads. While the world’s urban population continues to grow, cities are increasingly pressed by chronic and acute stresses like increasing inequity, polluted air and waters, limited governance and financial capacities, along with entrenched spasmodic crime and conflict – and the COVID-19 pandemic. Climate change has now exacerbated these problems and in many cases created new ones, at a time when cities are asked to be the bulwark of the climate solution space. ## Planning Sustainable Cities: Lessons from the COVID-19 Pandemic The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the fragility of urban systems but also revealed opportunities for transformation. It forced cities worldwide to rethink urban planning, particularly in terms of public space usage, transportation, and social infrastructure. The **drastic reduction in human activity** led to improved air quality and lower carbon emissions, demonstrating that behavioral changes can significantly impact urban sustainability. ### Addressing Urban Inequalities and Energy Poverty Research from Boza-Kiss et al. (2021) highlights how the **pandemic exacerbated existing inequalities in cities**. Lockdowns amplified urban energy poverty, disproportionately affecting lower-income groups living in poorly insulated homes. Increased energy use for heating and electricity raised costs, burdening vulnerable households. The crisis underscored the need for energy-efficient building renovations and social support mechanisms to ensure equitable access to energy. ### The Role of Urban Governance in Pandemic Resilience Findings from Chu et al. (2021) emphasize that urban governance capacity, rather than city size, plays a crucial role in pandemic resilience. Their study of **276 Chinese cities** found that well-governed cities had higher recovery rates and better pandemic control outcomes. Cities with robust governance structures benefited from better resource allocation, crisis management, and emergency response coordination. Investments in smart city technologies also enhanced cities’ ability to monitor and manage crises effectively. ![](https://www.uccrn.education/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/image-1.png) Urban governance strategies that integrate long-term planning, early warning systems, and coordinated action across multiple sectors can significantly enhance a city’s ability to respond to crises. Chu et al. (2021) further highlight that cities with advanced smart city infrastructures were able to leverage digital tools, artificial intelligence, and big data analytics to improve health monitoring and optimize resource distribution during the pandemic. ### The Digital Divide and Urban Inclusion The pandemic also exposed significant **digital divides** within urban areas. Many lower-income communities struggled with access to digital services, impacting remote work, education, and essential services. According to Boza-Kiss et al. (2021), improved digital infrastructure and inclusive policies can help bridge these gaps, ensuring that all citizens benefit from smart city technologies and online resources. Cities must prioritize investments in digital literacy programs and affordable broadband access to ensure equitable participation in the digital economy. ### Slum Vulnerabilities and Informal Settlements Slums and informal settlements were hit particularly hard by the pandemic, with overcrowding and inadequate access to sanitation making social distancing nearly impossible. The pandemic reinforced the urgency of slum upgrading programs that provide secure housing, **reliable infrastructure** and access to essential services. Cities must adopt community-driven planning approaches to enhance resilience and equity in these vulnerable areas. ![](https://www.uccrn.education/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/image.png) ### Future Directions for Sustainable Urban Development To create sustainable cities of the future, urban planners must integrate multiple strategies, including: Building sustainable cities of the future is a pressing priority. Abstract -------- Smart technologies enhance modern life but may introduce vulnerabilities to human and nature-induced disruptions due to their dependence on interconnected infrastructures. The vision of building smart yet resilient cities has taken precedence amid escalating threats from climate-related disturbances and infrastructure strains caused by rapid urbanization. Resilient smart cities embed adaptive capacities in civic systems to maintain operational continuity during disruptive events. Achieving this requires well-developed preparedness plans, coordinated emergency responses, and efficient recovery protocols. Unlike previous reviews that address smart cities and resilience separately, our study uniquely examines their critical intersection. Through systematic analysis of literature from eight major electronic databases, we identify key portfolios, paradigms, and frameworks that shape urban resilience while revealing a fundamental technology-resilience paradox insufficiently addressed in existing literature. We make several distinctive contributions: examining the complex relationship between 'smartness' and resilience; analyzing ten integrated portfolios across technical, social, and governance dimensions; and presenting practical case studies from diverse global contexts. Our findings reveal that successful implementation requires balancing technological innovation with resilient strategies such as hybrid solutions and resilience-by-design principles, reinforced by cohesive partnerships between government, businesses, and citizens. * [Previous article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000075) * [Next article in issue](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000269) Key words --------- Smart city Urban resilience Resilient smart cities Disaster management Infrastructure resilience 1. Introduction --------------- The global attention on sustainability, community resilience and smart cities initiatives within academic research, technological sectors, and civic domains is readily justifiable given their critical role in addressing current problems while preparing humanity to combat future social, environmental, and infrastructural challenges ([Koliou et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0001)). Worldwide migration is reaching unprecedented levels, with a continuous influx from rural to urban areas raising various concerns in the international community ([Swain, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0002)). These concerns reflect the need to upgrade city infrastructure and resources to accommodate the imminent surge in population. Thus, there is a pressing need for concerted urbanization efforts to address the looming disproportionate consumption of state resources—a situation that could prove dire in the future ([Donner & Rodríguez, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0003)). Estimates suggest that as of 2022, over half of the world's population resides in urban areas ([Ritchie et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0004)), despite these areas comprising only 1–3 % of the earth's surface ([Liu et al., 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0005)). Without effective measures, the quality of life may decline due to limited land area, dwindling fiscal conditions, traffic congestion, air pollution, inefficient resource utilization, and the potential spread of highly infectious diseases ([Zhang et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0006)). Consequently, consistent urban population increase will result in strained infrastructure, insecurity, food scarcity, insufficient public resources, and inadequate healthcare access ([Donner & Rodríguez, 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0003)). As cities become more densely populated and grapple with the demand for sustainable living standards, two potential solutions come to the forefront: * •Transforming rural regions into urban settlements. * •Elevating urban settlements into smart cities through the integration of information, and communication technologies (ICT) and Artificial Intelligence (AI) based innovations. Conversely, the need for smart cities transcends the challenges arising from urban population growth. It extends to the impacts of natural (earthquakes, tornadoes, floods, hurricanes, etc.) or human-induced (terrorism, arson, chemical spills, nuclear accidents, war etc.) disasters on critical infrastructures, human lives, and the provision of essential city services. This involves the broader task of reinforcing the physical and social component of the city against these threats ([Shahrabani & Apanavičienė, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0007)), giving rise to the concept of Resilient Smart Cities (RSC). RSC are technologically advanced urban areas designed to mitigate the effects of disruptions, demonstrating the capacity to adapt to and promptly recover from stress. Every year, natural disasters claim an average of 50,000 lives globally, as calculated over the past few decades ([Ritchie & Rosado, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0008)). In 2023 alone, natural hazards resulted in 86,473 deaths worldwide and impacted 93.1 million people ([Centre for Research on the Epidemiology of Disasters, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0009)). This issue is particularly critical in impoverished regions where inadequate and substandard housing infrastructure, along with low building standards, worsen the situation ([Choudhury et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0010)). In 2023, a devastating earthquake struck major residential areas in Turkey and Syria, resulting in reported casualties of over 45,000 lives in Turkey and >7000 lives in Syria ([Dal Zilio & Ampuero, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0011)). The earthquake led to the destruction of several buildings, impacting power lines, road networks, and mobile and wireless communication infrastructures. The significant loss of life and extensive damage following such disasters accentuate the critical need for robust fragility planning in rural and urban dwellings. In attaining the vision of RSC, municipalities must conduct comprehensive reviews to identify gaps and areas requiring additional financing and research. Resilience benchmarks need to be established and quantified for the long term, as today's resilience levels may prove insufficient in the future. After experiencing a crisis, cities must demonstrate improved resilience scores across indices compared to pre-crisis assessments ([Croope & McNeil, 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0012)). The post-event resilience level, as indicated by the pace and extent of restoration, should surpass the resilience observed prior to the disruption. Continuously implementing updates through modeling test beds following disruptions allows cities to elevate their systemic resilience rather than merely reverting to previous inadequacies ([Wang et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0013)). This continuous process of enhancing resilience based on lessons learned from each crisis is crucial for advancing urban readiness and response capacities over time. ### 1.1. **Structure and scope of the review** This study provides a cross-disciplinary perspective on the evolving narrative of RSC through a careful review of selected scholarly articles using the SALSA (Search, AppraisaL, Synthesis, and Analysis) framework ([Grant & Booth, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0014)). Our review makes five distinctive contributions to the field: * 1.We uniquely examine the critical intersection between smart cities and resilience, whereas most existing literature treats these domains separately. * 2.We identify and analyze a fundamental technology-resilience dilemma: how increasing technological sophistication can simultaneously enhance and potentially undermine urban resilience. * 3.We connect ten key portfolios of RSC across technical, social, and governance dimensions, offering a holistic view that transcends the technological focus of many existing reviews. * 4.We synthesize evidence on hybrid and resilience-by-design solutions that balance technological innovation with traditional systems to optimize resilience, highlighting effective approaches to navigating trade-offs between efficiency and redundancy. * 5.We offer stakeholder-specific guidance based on empirical evidence from diverse geographical contexts to help bridge the gap between theoretical frameworks and practical implementation. The paper is divided into five key sections, as shown in [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#fig0001). The first section explores the evolving concepts of smart cities, examining diverse definitions, core ideas, and attributes that characterize the contemporary urban landscape. This is followed by an assessment of practical resilience frameworks within smart cities, analyzing the intersection of these two concepts under the urban resilience subheading. The next section reviews the progress and applications of ten major RSC portfolios. Subsequently, we provide insights into the foundational elements necessary for successful RSC implementation. Finally, we examine the double-edged role of technology in RSC, a key challenge in their evolution. ![Image 11: Fig 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2664328625000324-gr1.jpg) 1. Sustainability and resilience are often discussed together in urban systems, yet they operate on different temporal and functional scales. While sustainability focuses on balancing resource consumption and regeneration over the long term ([Ruggerio, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0015)), resilience concerns a city's adaptive ability to withstand and recover from acute and chronic stresses while maintaining critical functions ([Meerow et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0016)). Given this distinction, our review systematically investigates the resilience of smart cities while acknowledging broader sustainability considerations. 2. Methodology -------------- This study employed a state-of-the-art literature review approach following the SALSA (Search, AppraisaL, Synthesis, and Analysis) framework ([Grant & Booth, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0014)), which provided a structured yet flexible methodology suitable for examining the evolving field of RSCs. Alternative approaches such as PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) have also been applied effectively to state-of-the-art reviews ([Huang et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0017)), however SALSA better supports our objective of capturing emerging developments in this dynamic field while maintaining methodological rigor. Search Phase. We gathered relevant literature through an iterative search process that embodied the flexibility inherent in state-of-the-art reviews. Following established approaches to complex literature reviews ([Grant & Booth, 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0014); [Snyder, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0018)), we began with foundational searches across eight electronic databases: Google Scholar, ScienceDirect, Springer, Web of Science, MDPI, Taylor & Francis, Scopus, and IEEE Xplore. Our initial searches employed broad conceptual terms (e.g., "smart city" AND "resilience" AND "resilient smart city"), which were progressively refined based on emerging connections and themes. For example, when early results revealed the importance of Internet of Things (IoT) and artificial intelligence (AI) in resilience strategy, we expanded our searches to include terms like "Internet of Things" ([Bellini et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0019)), and "artificial intelligence" ([Senarath et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0020)) and "urban resilience" ([Sharifi et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0021)). Similarly, after identifying the significance of human-centric approaches in modern cities, we incorporated search strings such as "citizen participation" ([Simonofski et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0022)), and "people-centric smart cities" ([Ji et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0023)), and "community resilience" ([Koliou et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0001)). This responsive approach allowed us to capture emerging concepts that might have been missed using rigid predefined criteria. [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#fig0002) shows our overall selection and screening process. Each article's abstract, keywords, introduction, figures, and conclusion underwent preliminary examination to assess relevance to the resilience and smart cities discourse. For instance, Kitchin's (2019) work on vulnerabilities in smart urbanism, discussed in Section 7, was identified during early searches, which then led us to explore critical perspectives on technology vulnerabilities ([Kitchin & Dodge, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0024)). Similarly, Meerow et al.'s (2016) conceptualization of urban resilience provided a theoretical foundation that informed subsequent literature identification ([Meerow et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0016)). ![Image 12: Fig 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2664328625000324-gr2.jpg) 1. Appraisal Phase. Building on the search results, we conducted an incisive appraisal by examining each article's abstract, keywords, introduction, figures, and conclusion to assess relevance to the resilience and smart cities discourse. To ensure systematic evaluation, we developed five key research questions: * 1.Does the literature sufficiently address at least one key portfolio or dimension of resilient smart cities? * 2.What research conclusions help understand the current state-of-the-art in resilient smart cities? * 3.Does the literature explore the intersection and/or balance of smart city concepts and resilience in urban contexts? * 4.How does the literature address urban resilience within disaster and sustainability criteria? * 5.What gaps or challenges in adopting smart technologies in smart cities does the paper identify, and how do these gaps affect a city's resilience? Synthesis and Analysis Phases. Our synthesis and analysis phase employed a thematic analysis approach that developed and evolved through four sequential stages as new literature was incorporated. First, we conducted content exploration of papers as they were identified through our search and appraisal process. This ongoing analysis allowed us to recognize recurring concepts across the literature. For example, as papers discussing "infrastructure resilience" were identified, we noted how this concept was approached differently across technological domains; from physical redundancy in transportation systems ([Lacinák, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0025)) to digital fail-safes in smart grids ([Gazijahani et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0026)). Second, we developed a thematic framework by categorizing these recurring concepts into broader themes. This process revealed ten key portfolios of RSC (presented in Section 5) and several cross-cutting themes. For instance, when analyzing smart governance literature, we identified recurring emphasis on both vertical integration (government-led initiatives) and horizontal coordination (multi-stakeholder collaboration), which informed our discussion of collaborative foundations in Section 6. Third, we conducted comparative analysis to identify patterns, trends, and inconsistencies. This revealed important evolution in how resilience concepts have developed over time; from traditional infrastructure-focused approaches toward more integrated socio-technical frameworks. For example, we traced how early literature often treated technology as separate from social resilience, while more recent work increasingly emphasizes their interdependence. Finally, we identified research gaps and emerging directions through our ongoing analysis. This examination revealed several knowledge gaps, particularly regarding long-term evaluation of resilience outcomes, resilience-by-design approaches and integration of hybrid technological solutions. This thematic approach enabled us to synthesize the literature into a coherent conceptual framework. ### 2.1. **Quality assurance** Verification of findings occurred continuously throughout our analysis, with particular attention to cross-referencing against established frameworks and primary sources. We revisited primary studies to verify that our thematic interpretations remained consistent with the original source materials. For example, when synthesizing perspectives on technology vulnerabilities in Section 7, we returned to key papers ([Kitchin & Dodge, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0024); [Cañavera-Herrera et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0027)) to ensure our integration of their findings accurately preserved their original context and conclusions. Our analysis was further strengthened by corroborating our findings with established frameworks described in [Section 4.2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#sec0012), such as the PEOPLES resilience framework ([Renschler et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0028)), the DICES framework ([Mourshed et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0029)), and the City Resilience Framework (CRF) developed by Arup ([Arup, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0030)). This cross-validation process helped ensure that our identification of key dimensions aligned with recognized standards while also highlighting where emerging research extends beyond conventional frameworks. Additionally, we maintained awareness of the geographic and contextual diversity represented across these frameworks, from the SPUR framework for the San Francisco Bay area ([San Francisco Planning and Urban Research Association (SPUR) 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0031)) to international standards ([UNISDR, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#bib0032)). This allowed us to recognize patterns and principles that transcend specific urban contexts while remaining sensitive to the importance of local implementation factors. ### 2.2. **Bibliometric analysis of RSC literature** To understand publication trends in RSC, we conducted a comprehensive Scopus database search with the following criteria. Our search string combined core smart city terms ((smart OR intelligent OR digital) NEAR/3 (city OR cities OR urban)) with resilience-related concepts (resilient, resilience, disaster, urban challenge, infrastructure vulnerability, emergency response, adaptive capacity, preparedness, recovery, disruption). We applied specific inclusion criteria: peer-reviewed articles and books published between 2000 and 2024, English language publications, and works addressing both smart city and resilience concepts. Single-domain applications without urban context were excluded, as were non-peer-reviewed materials including editorials, letters, notes, and errata. This structured search produced 4143 records traversing multiple subject areas, as illustrated in [Fig. 3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#fig0003), [Fig. 4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2664328625000324#fig0004). ![Image 13: Fig 3](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2664328625000324-gr3.jpg) 1. enriched_points.expanded_content: Smart cities enhance resilience by integrating technology and innovative practices, enabling them to withstand and quickly recover from environmental and social disruptions, thus maintaining functionality.
Smart city planning aligns with global sustainability objectives, promoting eco-friendly practices, resource efficiency, and social equity to ensure the well-being of present and future generations.
By embracing cutting-edge technologies and fostering collaboration, smart cities position themselves as leaders in urban innovation, driving economic growth and attracting talent and investment.

Integrating smart technologies improves public safety through advanced surveillance, real-time data analytics, and efficient emergency response systems, ensuring a safer urban environment for all residents.

enriched_points.point_title: A Model for Future Urban Planning
enriched_points.expanded_content: Smart cities serve as benchmarks for future urban planning, showcasing best practices in sustainability, resilience, and citizen engagement, ultimately guiding global urban development strategies.
